one_line_takeaway: Spa-VLM shows that text-only RAG poisoning is defeated by the visual-retrieval stage of multimodal RAG, but jointly crafting imperceptibly-perturbed images plus reranker-aligned misleading text lets just 5 injected entries drive >0.8 attack success against RAG-based VLMs while evading three standard defenses.
key_finding: With only N=5 malicious paired entries per target question, Spa-VLM achieves ASR 0.87 (Precision 0.85) on Encyclopedic-VQA and ASR 0.83 (Precision 0.84) on InfoSeek, despite knowledge bases of 2M and 100K entries respectively (a poisoning ratio around 1 in hundreds of thousands to millions). Adapted single-modal baselines essentially fail (PoisonedRAG 0.02 ASR on E-VQA, 0.01 on InfoSeek; Corpus Poisoning 0.0/0.01; Naive paired-but-separated 0.0). The strongest baseline, Prompt Injection, reached only 0.63 (E-VQA) and 0.46 (InfoSeek). All three tested defenses (randomized input preprocessing, target-question paraphrasing, SHA-256 duplicate-text filtering) failed to reduce ASR, with some cases even slightly increasing it.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Spa-VLM, presented as the first knowledge-base data-poisoning attack targeting RAG-based Vision-Language Models (VLMs) used for Visual Question Answering. Its central claim is that unimodal RAG poisoning methods designed for text-only RAG-LLMs fail completely in the multimodal setting (0% ASR observed when applied directly), because a multimodal RAG pipeline first retrieves knowledge entries by VISUAL similarity to the query image, so text-only poison is never retrieved. Spa-VLM's own contribution is a paired image+text poisoning paradigm: for each target image-question pair the attacker crafts N malicious knowledge entries, each containing (1) a poisoned image carrying imperceptible PGD adversarial noise optimized so its Eva-CLIP embedding is close to the target image class (satisfying the visual retrieval stage), and (2) a poisoned text optimized both for reranker similarity (via a Q-Former fused-embedding loss) and for 'aggressiveness' (iteratively rewritten by a VLM so that, used as context, it steers the LLM to emit the attacker's target answer). Injecting these paired entries makes the RAG-based VLM produce attacker-chosen wrong answers (e.g., labeling a Nazi flag as a symbol of 'courage', or a protected animal as edible).
threat_model: White-box knowledge-base poisoning. The attacker can inject a small number (N=5 per target question) of image-text entries into the external Wikipedia-derived knowledge base the RAG-based VLM retrieves from (motivated by the cited finding that ~6.5% of Wikipedia documents can be maliciously edited). The attacker is assumed to KNOW the visual retriever and text reranker parameters (justified via Kerckhoffs's principle and the public availability of these open components), but does not need to modify or access the underlying LLM's weights. The attacker chooses a target image class, a set of target text questions, and a specific incorrect target answer R per question; success is the LLM's output containing R for such queries. The paper itself flags white-box access to the retriever/reranker as a limitation and leaves black-box extension to future work.
limitations: Author-acknowledged: (1) White-box assumption on retriever and reranker parameters; black-box generalization is not validated. (2) Only three basic filtering defenses are examined, so the claim that defenses are 'insufficient' is limited in scope. Reviewer-noted from the text: the strong ASR/Precision numbers depend heavily on the small default k1=5 visual-retrieval window; the paper's own Table III shows ASR degrades as more clean entries are retrieved (down to ~0.65 at k1>=15-20 and k1=50), so a defender simply widening retrieval breadth measurably weakens the attack. The 'stealth' of adversarial images (epsilon=0.05 PGD) is asserted qualitatively without a human-detectability or perceptual study. The attack requires white-box gradient access to the exact Eva-CLIP/Q-Former encoders, and the LLM-side 'aggressiveness' loop depends on a capable VLM (GPT-4o/InternVL2-8B) to author convincing misleading text.

datasets_used: Encyclopedic-VQA (E-VQA): 221K QA pairs, controlled multimodal knowledge base of 2M Wikipedia articles with images
InfoSeek: 1.3M visual information-seeking questions, 100K Wikipedia-article knowledge base (KB released by prior work EchoSight)
Evaluation used 200 closed-ended target questions per dataset, repeated 10x = 2000 unique target questions
quantitative_results: Spa-VLM (full, with reranker) ASR/Precision: E-VQA 0.87/0.85, InfoSeek 0.83/0.84
Spa-VLM without reranker ASR/Precision: 0.83/0.82 on both E-VQA and InfoSeek
N=5 malicious paired entries per target question is enough to exceed 0.8 ASR against 2M (E-VQA) and 100K (InfoSeek) entry KBs
Baseline PoisonedRAG ASR: 0.02 (E-VQA), 0.01 (InfoSeek); Corpus Poisoning 0.0/0.01; Naive Attack 0.0/0.0
Directly applying existing single-modal RAG-LLM poisoning to VLM yielded 0% ASR (stated as motivation)
k1 ablation (Table III): ASR falls as retrieval window widens, stabilizing near 0.65 at k1>=15 (Eva-CLIP: 0.83@k1=5 -> 0.65@k1=50; OpenAI-CLIP: 0.85@k1=5 -> 0.65@k1=50)
Defenses (Table V) all ineffective: preprocessing E-VQA 0.87->0.88, InfoSeek 0.83->0.86; paraphrasing E-VQA 0.87->0.83, InfoSeek 0.83->0.85; duplicate filtering unchanged (0.87, 0.83)
Attack efficiency (Table II): <3 VLM queries per malicious text (E-VQA 2.59 img / 4.43 text queries; InfoSeek 2.74 / 4.51), image-similarity optimization ~3-4 s per entry
Attack hyperparameters: PGD epsilon=0.05, t=40 iterations; text length V=50 words; max text-optimization attempts L=10; InternVL2-8B temperature=1
baselines_compared: Naive Attack (malicious image and text injected as separate entries, not paired)
Prompt Injection Attack (instruction injected into LLM prompt)
Corpus Poisoning Attack (Zhong et al. 2023, random-character adversarial passages)
PoisonedRAG (Zou et al. 2024, text-only knowledge poisoning for RAG-LLMs)
